s: Twelve layers of the dead, stacked where land ran out
w: Space was so scarce in the old ghetto that bodies were buried twelve deep — some 100,000 graves pressed into a half-hectare, their stones tilting and shouldering each other across the centuries. Active from the early 15th century until 1786, it holds the Maharal, the rabbi said to have created the Golem. One of Europe's most haunting sites.
_facts: Served as the Jewish community's burial ground from the early 15th century to 1786
An estimated 100,000 burials in layers up to twelve deep due to severe land restrictions in the ghetto
Notable burials include Rabbi Judah Loew ben Bezalel (the Maharal, c. 1526–1609), credited in legend with creating the Golem of Prague
Mordecai Meisel (1528–1601) and historian David Gans (c. 1541–1613) are also buried here
Now administered by the Jewish Museum in Prague

s: Medieval clockwork that once scared its maker blind — allegedly
w: Built in 1410, this is the oldest working astronomical clock in the world. Legend says the city council had its maker, Master Hanus, blinded so he could never replicate it elsewhere — whereupon he stopped the mechanism from inside, and it took a century to restart. The hourly parade of apostles still draws crowds every time.
_facts: The Prague Orloj dates to 1410, making it the third-oldest astronomical clock in the world and the oldest still in operation
It displays the position of the Sun and Moon, astronomical time, and more on its medieval face
The legend of master clockmaker Hanus being blinded to prevent him replicating the clock is widely told but historically unverified
The parade of apostolic figures through the windows above the clock face occurs on the hour

s: Communist-era needle spiked with giant crawling babies
w: Built between 1985 and 1992 on a hill where a Jewish cemetery once stood, the 216-metre brutalist transmitter was one of the most reviled structures in post-war Prague. Then David Černý fixed it — by attaching ten fibreglass babies, each with a barcode for a face, clambering up the shaft. They stayed. The tower now has a hotel room inside it.
_facts: Constructed 1985–1992 to designs by architect Václav Aulický; it stands 216 m tall
Built on the site of a former Jewish cemetery in the Žižkov district
Artist David Černý added ten faceless crawling-baby sculptures (miminka) to the tower in 2000; they were made permanent
The tower houses an observation deck and a boutique hotel pod-room
Voted one of the world's ugliest buildings in various polls, and also one of the most distinctive

s: Brewing the same dark lager since 1499
w: One of the oldest brewpubs in Europe — the licence to brew on this site dates to 1499 and has never lapsed. The dark 13-degree lager produced in the courtyard brewery is not sold anywhere else in the world. Sit in the vaulted hall, ignore the tourist schnapps-pushers, and drink it at the source.
_facts: U Fleků has been brewing beer on the same site in the New Town since records show 1499
Located at Křemencova 11, Prague 1, not far from the National Theatre
The dark lager (tmavé pivo) is brewed on site and sold exclusively on the premises
The building complex surrounds a central courtyard; the facade features a highly decorated clock

s: Where the nation buries its geniuses — Dvořák, Mucha, Čapek
w: Prague's Pantheon: a walled graveyard inside the ancient Vyšehrad fort where Czech artists, writers and composers have been interred since 1869. Dvořák and Smetana lie here, as do Alphonse Mucha, Karel Čapek and the sculptor Josef Václav Myslbek. The centrepiece is the Slavín collective tomb, a mausoleum holding fifty of the country's greatest names.
_facts: Founded in 1869 as a national cemetery within Vyšehrad Castle grounds
Burials include Antonín Dvořák, Bedřich Smetana, Karel Čapek, Alphonse Mucha, and Jan Neruda
The Slavín collective tomb was designed by Antonín Wiehl and holds around 50 prominent Czechs
The cemetery is administered as a national monument

s: Two Baroque halls so beautiful photography barely does them justice
w: The Premonstratensian abbey founded in 1143 shelters two extraordinary library halls: the Theological Hall (1679) with ceiling frescoes and globes packed into every alcove, and the Philosophical Hall (1794) built around a vast trompe-l'oeil ceiling. Over 200,000 volumes line the walls. Visitors peer in from the doorway — books this old demand no handling.
_facts: Strahov Monastery was founded in 1143 by Jindřich Zdík, Bishop of Olomouc, under Vladislaus II
The Theological Hall dates from 1679, the Philosophical Hall from 1794; both have ornate ceiling frescoes
The library holds over 200,000 volumes including manuscripts and early printed books
The abbey is Premonstratensian; it survived Communist suppression by operating as a state museum

s: 77,297 names hand-painted on the walls — every Czech Jewish victim
w: The second-oldest surviving synagogue in Prague, completed in 1535, became after the war the most harrowing memorial in the city: the names, birth dates, and death dates of 77,297 Bohemian and Moravian Jewish Holocaust victims are inscribed by hand across every interior wall. A children's art exhibition from Terezín fills the upper floor. Almost no one comes out unmoved.
_facts: Completed in 1535, the Pinkas Synagogue is the second-oldest surviving synagogue in Prague
After World War II it was converted into a memorial: 77,297 names of Czech Jewish Holocaust victims were painted on the walls
The Communists had the inscriptions whitewashed in 1968; they were painstakingly restored after 1989
An exhibition of children's drawings from Terezín concentration camp is displayed upstairs
Now administered by the Jewish Museum in Prague

s: Where Czechoslovakia declared independence — and Art Nouveau peaked
w: On 28 October 1918 the independent Republic of Czechoslovakia was declared in this building. Opened in 1912 on the site of a demolished royal palace, it is the zenith of Prague Art Nouveau: every surface encrusted with mosaics, stucco, and allegory by Mucha, Preisler, and Švabinský. The grand concert hall beneath its glass dome is named for Smetana.
_facts: Built 1905–1912 to designs by Osvald Polívka and Antonín Balšánek on the site of the demolished Royal Court
The Czechoslovak declaration of independence was proclaimed here on 28 October 1918
Interior decoration by Alfons Mucha, Jan Preisler, Max Švabinský, and Ladislav Šaloun
Smetana Hall is the main concert venue; the building also has a café, French restaurant, and basement wine bar
Named a national cultural monument; the style is Czech Art Nouveau

s: Prague's oldest standing structure — a pebble of Romanesque stone
w: This squat circular chapel is the oldest intact building in Prague, dating from the 11th century when Vyšehrad was a rival seat of power to the castle across the river. It was used as a powder magazine and then sealed for centuries before being restored in the 1870s. From outside it looks like a rounded gatehouse; inside it barely fits a handful of worshippers.
_facts: The Rotunda of St. Martin at Vyšehrad dates from the 11th century, making it the oldest surviving building in Prague
Vyšehrad itself was founded likely in the 10th century as an alternative royal seat
The rotunda was used as a gunpowder store in later centuries and sealed; restored in the 1870s
It represents the Romanesque style, predating most of the Gothic and Baroque city that surrounds it

s: Hidden terraced baroque garden behind an unmarked gate
w: Through a discreet door in Karmelitská Street a steep Baroque garden climbs the slope of Petřín Hill in three terraced levels — designed by František Maxmilián Kaňka in the 1710s for the Count of Vrtba, decorated with original stone sculptures by Matyáš Bernard Braun. UNESCO-listed; almost always quieter than anywhere else in Malá Strana.
_facts: Laid out in the 1710s–1720s by architect František Maxmilián Kaňka for Jan Josef, Count of Vrtba
Original Baroque statuary and sculptural decor by sculptor Matyáš Bernard Braun has survived
The Sala Terrena linking palace and garden has frescoes by Václav Vavřinec Reiner
Listed by UNESCO for its historic cultural values; the garden was restored between 1990 and 1998
Entered through a barely visible gate in Karmelitská Street

s: The spot where two students burned themselves for freedom
w: A small bronze plaque and eternal flame mark where Jan Palach set himself alight on 16 January 1969 — a 20-year-old student protesting the Soviet crushing of the Prague Spring. He died three days later. A month after, Jan Zajíc did the same in the same square. The memorial is understated, which makes it more powerful.
_facts: Jan Palach, a student of Charles University, self-immolated at the top of Wenceslas Square on 16 January 1969 in protest at the Soviet-led Warsaw Pact invasion of 1968
He died on 19 January 1969 at age 20
Jan Zajíc followed suit on 25 February 1969, also in Wenceslas Square
The spot is marked by a small cross inlaid in the pavement and a memorial plaque at the base of the National Museum steps

s: The world's only functioning Cubist café — in Old Town
w: Josef Gočár built this six-storey Cubist building on Celetná in 1912 — the first and arguably finest example of Czech Cubist architecture. The first floor retains the Grand Café Orient, restored to its original angular Cubist design. Above it, the Museum of Czech Cubism. Below street level, a tiny Baroque statuette of the Black Madonna persists in a corner niche, giving the house its name.
_facts: Designed by Josef Gočár and completed in 1912; considered the earliest example of Cubist architecture in Prague
The Grand Café Orient on the first floor is restored to its original angular Cubist interior design
The four upper floors house the Museum of Czech Cubism (part of the National Gallery)
Named after the small Baroque Black Madonna statuette in a corner niche, a remnant of the previous building on the site
Building fully restored in 2003 after decades of alteration

s: Europe's oldest active synagogue, home to the Golem's legend
w: Completed around 1270, this is the oldest working synagogue in Europe and one of the earliest Gothic buildings in Prague. The attic, say the stories, is where Rabbi Loew stored the lifeless body of his Golem between activations — and where it remains, untouched, to this day. The pointed brick gable and the spare Gothic interior have barely changed in 750 years.
_facts: Completed c. 1270; Europe's oldest active synagogue and one of the earliest Gothic buildings in Prague
Built in the twin-nave Gothic style; the oldest surviving medieval synagogue of this type
The legend of Rabbi Judah Loew ben Bezalel's Golem holds that the clay figure's remains are stored in the attic
Still functions as an Orthodox Jewish congregation

s: Communist Prague's only legal act of defiance — a dead pop star
w: After John Lennon was killed in 1980, an unknown artist painted his face on this wall on Velkopřevorské náměstí. Under Communism, every coat of paint the authorities applied was redone overnight. The wall became a pilgrimage site for young Czechs. It was repainted entirely after the Velvet Revolution, and has kept evolving since — slogan, tag, flower, repeat.
_facts: Located on Velkopřevorské náměstí, Malá Strana, facing the wall of the Grand Priory of the Knights of Malta
A mural of John Lennon appeared after his murder in 1980; Communist authorities repeatedly whitewashed it, it was always repainted
Became a site of free expression and protest under the regime
Fully repainted after 1989 and continues to evolve as living graffiti
Has inspired similar 'Lennon Walls' globally, notably in Hong Kong during the 2019 protests

s: The world's largest metronome, on the plinth where Stalin once stood
w: The largest Stalin monument ever built stood here from 1955 until 1962 — a 30-metre granite procession of Soviet worker-types led by the man himself, demolished on Khrushchev's orders after de-Stalinisation. The empty plinth was eventually filled in 1991 by a 23-metre red metronome by Vratislav Novák. It swings. A plaque at the base reads: 'In time, all things pass.'
_facts: The Stalin Monument in Prague, unveiled 1955, was the largest Stalin statue in the world at the time; demolished 1962 after Khrushchev's de-Stalinisation
The plinth in Letná Park stood empty for decades; artists and skateboarders claimed the space
The Prague Metronome (Pražský metronom) was erected in 1991 by artist Vratislav Novák; officially named 'Time Machine'
Standing 23 m (75 ft) tall, it may be the largest metronome in the world
The site offers panoramic views over the Old Town and the river bend

s: Gehry's warped glass tower — Fred and Ginger on the Vltava
w: Between stately 19th-century facades on the Rašín Embankment, a glass tower appears to lean and swivel at the waist — two cylinders like a dancing pair. Frank Gehry (with Czech architect Vlado Milunić) designed it in 1992; completed 1996. Gehry called it 'Ginger and Fred', then worried he was 'importing Hollywood kitsch to Prague' and quietly dropped the nickname.
_facts: Designed by Frank Gehry and Vlado Milunić, 1992; construction completed 1996
Built on a riverfront plot that had been a bomb site (from a 1945 USAF raid) until the 1990s
Gehry nicknamed it 'Ginger and Fred' after the dancers Ginger Rogers and Fred Astaire; the name stuck for the rooftop restaurant
One of the few deconstructivist buildings in Central Europe; now a National Cultural Monument
Houses the Dancing House Hotel and a rooftop bar with Vltava views

s: Kafka's grave, in the cemetery built to replace the one under the TV tower
w: Opened in 1890 specifically to relieve pressure on the old Žižkov Jewish cemetery — the site that was later bulldozed to build the television tower. Franz Kafka is buried here, in a modest joined tombstone with his parents. Visitors leave small stones and paper messages. The cemetery is dense with ivy and quiet in a way central Prague almost never is.
_facts: Established 1890 to replace the overcrowded Old Jewish Cemetery in Žižkov; the original site was later used for the TV tower
Franz Kafka (1883–1924) is buried here with his parents Hermann and Julie
The cemetery covers a large wooded area in Žižkov (Praha 3); declared a cultural monument in 1964
Visitors traditionally leave small stones on Jewish graves as a mark of respect

s: A Jesuit fortress of knowledge with a Baroque hall from a fairy tale
w: The Jesuits built this sprawling complex on the bank of the Vltava in the 17th century — today it houses the National Library of the Czech Republic. The Baroque Library Hall inside (tours only) is all gilded stucco, theological globes, and ceiling frescoes; an astronomical observatory tower rises above it. One of the most beautiful rooms in any library, anywhere.
_facts: The Jesuit College of the Clementinum was established in the 16th–17th centuries; today houses the National Library of the Czech Republic
The Baroque Library Hall (Barokní sál) dates from the early 18th century and contains theological globes and ceiling frescoes
An astronomical observatory in the tower was used for continuous meteorological and astronomical records from 1775
The complex is one of the largest building complexes in Central Europe after Prague Castle
Access to the Baroque Hall and observatory is by guided tour only

s: Tiny alchemists' lane inside the castle walls — where Kafka wrote
w: Built in the 16th century to house Rudolf II's castle guard, this alley of miniature brightly painted cottages tucked under the castle fortification walls later became home to goldsmiths, then to the poor. Franz Kafka rented number 22 (his sister's house) in 1916–1917 and wrote there. It is now a museum street; the cottages contain arms, torture devices, and period rooms.
_facts: Originally built in the late 16th century to house Rudolf II's castle guard; later occupied by goldsmiths (hence 'Golden Lane')
Franz Kafka rented No. 22 from his sister Ottla in 1916–1917 and did much of his writing there
The lane of tiny coloured houses runs along the inside of the northern castle wall (Bílá věž / White Tower end)
Today the cottages are restored as small museum spaces covering medieval arms, 20th-century history, and daily life

s: The greatest Baroque church north of the Alps — and a Cold War spy post
w: Built between 1704 and 1755 on the former site of a 13th-century Gothic church, St. Nicholas is considered the supreme achievement of Prague Baroque — its dome and bell-tower dominate the Malá Strana skyline. During the Cold War, the tower was used by the StB (Communist secret police) to surveil the West German embassy opposite. Mozart played the organ here in 1787.
_facts: Constructed 1704–1755 by architects Christoph Dientzenhofer and his son Kilian Ignaz Dientzenhofer
Described as the greatest example of Prague Baroque and one of the finest Baroque churches in Central Europe
Mozart played the organ here on 4 October 1787 shortly before the premiere of Don Giovanni in Prague
The bell tower was used by the Czechoslovak secret police (StB) during the Cold War to monitor the nearby West German embassy
The dome (75 m) and bell tower are defining features of the Malá Strana skyline

s: A replica of the Virgin's house from Nazareth — and a diamond-studded monstrance
w: Built from 1626, this pilgrimage complex in Hradčany is a Baroque frame around a copy of the Santa Casa — the supposed Nazareth home of the Virgin Mary, replicated across Catholic Europe. The carillon of 27 bells, ringing since 1695, plays a Marian hymn every hour. In the treasury: the 'Diamond Monstrance' of 1699, its rays set with 6,222 diamonds.
_facts: Construction began 1626; blessed in 1631; financed by noblewoman Kateřina Benigna of the Lobkowicz family
The Santa Casa (Holy Hut) is a replica of the house in Nazareth where the Annunciation is said to have occurred; a Counter-Reformation pilgrimage concept replicated in dozens of European churches
The carillon of 27 bells has been playing a Marian chime every hour since 15 August 1695
The treasury holds the Prague Sun monstrance (1699), set with 6,222 diamonds
The Baroque facade was designed by Christoph Dientzenhofer and Kilian Ignaz Dientzenhofer in the early 18th century

s: A shrivelled arm hangs by the door — the thief who touched the Madonna
w: The longest nave of any church in Prague, rebuilt in Baroque extravagance after 1689, contains one of the city's strangest relics: a mummified forearm hanging to the right of the entrance, over 400 years old. The legend says a jewel thief reached for the Virgin Mary's necklace at the high altar — and Mary grabbed his arm and would not let go, so the monks cut it off. It has hung there ever since.
_facts: The Basilica of St. James the Greater dates from the 13th century; rebuilt in Baroque style after a fire in 1689
The church has the longest nave in Prague
A mummified forearm hangs near the entrance; the legend states it belonged to a jewel thief who tried to steal from the statue of the Virgin Mary at the high altar
The tomb of Count Vratislav of Mitrovice (died 1712) by Johann Bernhard Fischer von Erlach is one of the finest Baroque tombs in Bohemia
The Franciscan basilica also houses a celebrated pipe organ dating to 1705
